guide_description: One day anchored in the elevated arc from Los Feliz to Hollywood - an observatory with the best panorama in the city, the walk of fame as cultural artifact rather than tourist trap, and a cinema palace that puts every multiplex on earth to shame. The cluster rewards a car-free morning and an early start.

days.cluster_tagline: The hillside observatory, the city grid below, and the original dream factory boulevard
days.neighborhood_description: The spine running from Griffith Park down into Hollywood is the part of Los Angeles that most visitors picture before they arrive - and one of the few areas where the mythology and the physical reality converge. Griffith Observatory sits at 1,134 feet above sea level with unobstructed views across the basin to the Pacific. Hollywood Boulevard three miles south holds the Walk of Fame, the Egyptian, the El Capitan, and the Chinese Theatre on a single walkable stretch.

morning.notes: Drive or take the DASH Observatory bus from Los Feliz Boulevard - the parking lot fills completely by 10am on weekends and most clear weekdays. The rooftop terrace on the east side gives the cleaner view toward downtown; the west terrace is better for the Hollywood Sign framing. Admission to the building and planetarium shows is free; the Samuel Oschin Planetarium requires a timed ticket purchased at the door.

afternoon.notes: The hand and footprint forecourt outside is free to enter and worth lingering in - find Humphrey Bogart's cigarette-in-hand impression and the famously tiny prints of Judy Garland. Avoid paying for the costumed character photos clustered at the entrance; walk past them directly to the cement blocks. The interior lobby is accessible during screenings, which is the cheapest way to see the 1927 auditorium with its original dragon-beam ceiling.

evening.activity_title: Hollywood Walk of Fame and El Capitan Theatre
evening.notes: Walk the two-block stretch between Highland and Orange Drive for the highest density of names most people recognize - the Walk extends nearly four kilometers in each direction but this central section near the Chinese Theatre is the curated core. The El Capitan Theatre directly across the boulevard is a 1926 Spanish Colonial Revival house now operated by Disney; its neon marquee is one of the best-preserved on the boulevard and glows well after dark. Sunset turns the Hollywood Hills behind the Sign from brown to amber.

if_time_permits.notes: Frank Lloyd Wright's first Los Angeles commission (1921), sitting inside Barnsdall Art Park on Olive Hill. The hollyhock motifs cast into the concrete are Wright's earliest experiment with the textile block system he would develop throughout the 1920s. Tours run hourly Wednesday through Sunday; arrive at the park entrance on Hollywood Boulevard and walk up the hill rather than trying to park inside the complex.

The hilltop perimeter path around Hollyhock House gives a 360-degree view that includes the Hollywood Sign to the northwest and the downtown skyline to the southeast - a pairing rarely seen from a single vantage point. Free to enter the park; Hollyhock House tour requires a separate ticket.
